prompt: Explain the attention mechanism in transformer models to a software engineer who has never done ML. Keep it to about 200 words.
response:
Think of attention as a smart routing step that decides how much every word (or token) in a sequence should influence each other word when building a new representation.

Analogy: you have a set of "queries" (questions) and a table of (key, value) rows. For each query you:
- compare the query to every key to get a similarity score (usually a dot product),
- normalize those scores into weights (softmax) so they sum to 1,
- use the weights to compute a weighted sum of the values — that weighted sum is the query’s new context-aware representation.

In transformers this runs for every token: each token produces a query, key, and value vector (via learned linear projections). Self-attention means queries, keys, and values all come from the same sequence, so every token can attend to any other token. The result captures contextual relationships (short- and long-range) in parallel across tokens.

Key practical points:
- Multi-head attention runs several attention "heads" in parallel with different projections to capture different relationship types, then concatenates them.
- Attention is highly parallelizable (unlike RNNs) but has O(n^2) cost in sequence length due to all-pairs comparisons.
- Positional information is added separately because attention itself is order-agnostic.
